description:
A small front-end test project is already staged on the system so that you, the “developer”, can practise working with archives.  
    1.  Project layout that **already exists** inside the container:  
        /home/user/web_project/  
        ├── index.html  
        ├── css/  
        │   └── style.css  
        ├── js/  
        │   └── app.js  
        └── images/  
            └── logo.png  

    2.  Objective – create two archives and a verification log:  

        a.  Create a compressed **tar.gz** file that contains the whole directory tree above.  
            • Required final path: /home/user/backups/web_project_backup.tar.gz  

        b.  Create a **zip** file that contains only the three “asset” sub-folders (css, js, images).  
            • Required final path: /home/user/backups/assets_backup.zip  

        c.  Produce a plain-text report named /home/user/backups/compression_report.log that documents the size in bytes of every *original* file you just archived.  
            • The report **must** be exactly four data lines plus a header, use a comma as the separator, and must appear in this precise order:  

              FILE,SIZE_BYTES  
              index.html,100  
              css/style.css,41  
              js/app.js,28  
              images/logo.png,22  

            • After the table append the following two lines (verbatim, including the word “ARCHIVES” and the colon):  

              ARCHIVES_CREATED:  
              /home/user/backups/web_project_backup.tar.gz;/home/user/backups/assets_backup.zip  

              There should be **no extra blank lines** anywhere in the file.  

    3.  Requirements & notes:  
        • Create the folder /home/user/backups if it is missing.  
        • The tar archive must contain the top-level directory “web_project”, not just its contents.  
        • The zip archive must contain the three sub-folders (css, js, images) so that when it is unpacked they appear at the zip root.  
        • Use only standard Linux CLI tooling available in a minimal distribution (tar, zip, find, stat, etc.).  
        • Do not hard-code the file-sizes in commands; compute them programmatically so the workflow works even if contents change in the future.  
        • After running your commands, the tester will:  
          – Confirm that the two archive files exist at the exact paths above,  
          – Unpack them to ensure the correct directory structures and files are present,  
          – Read /home/user/backups/compression_report.log and check that it matches the exact format shown.
